questions: A widely used introductory criminal law textbook was published in 2015 by a Midwestern university libraries publishing program under a CC BY-NC-SA license. Focusing on books from the computing and information technology domain that share the same publisher, year (2015), and Creative Commons license as that criminal law text, identify the relevant title and describe its intended academic audience and subject focus. Additionally, explain the publicly stated process for suggesting edits or additional content to a book record on that publishing platform, including where users are directed if they want to improve a book itself or have questions about a title.

A widely used 2015 undergraduate criminal law textbook (ISBN 9781946135087) was published by a university-based open publishing initiative in Minnesota. Focusing only on other textbooks released by the same publisher within the fields of journalism, media studies, or communication, identify all such titles and explain for each: (1) its publication year and (2) its central thematic focus or pedagogical emphasis as described by the publisher.

Within the same publishing program that produced a well-known open undergraduate textbook titled *Introduction to Criminal Law* (released in 2015 by a university-based libraries publishing initiative), identify the computer science or information systems textbook issued by that same publisher. For this identified book, provide its full title, copyright year, licensing terms, and the intended academic audience or course context. Additionally, according to the hosting platform’s general guidelines for improving book records, explain the recommended process for suggesting an edit or adding content to a book’s record, including whom to contact for substantive content changes and where to direct general questions.

Identify the open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook that uses a two-step “applied approach” (introducing legal concepts in components and then reinforcing them through factual examples), includes 14 chapters ending with an appendix of case listings, and is widely adopted in introductory courses. What is its publisher, copyright year, ISBN-13, Creative Commons license type, available online format(s), and how many published faculty reviews are associated with it on its primary open textbook listing page?

Identify the open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook published in 2015 by a major Midwestern U.S. university library press that uses a two-step “applied approach” to teaching doctrine (breaking crimes into elements and then applying them to fact patterns) and is organized into 14 chapters covering topics from constitutional protections to crimes against the government. Once you have identified the correct textbook, provide the following details: its ISBN-13, publisher name, copyright year, primary language, licensing terms, available online format(s), and the total number of published peer reviews associated with it.

An open-access introductory Criminal Law textbook published in 2015 by a university library press under a CC BY-NC-SA license is hosted on a larger open textbook platform. On that same platform, users are given a way to propose corrections or additions to a book’s bibliographic record. What mechanism does the platform provide for suggesting changes to a book record, and what instructions are given to users who want to improve a book’s content itself? In addition, within the platform’s Computer Science catalog, identify the 2014 Spanish-language introductory book on Artificial Intelligence authored by three contributors. For that AI title, state its full title, publisher, license, named contributors, and summarize the major thematic areas it covers.

Identify the open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook published in 2015 by a major Midwestern U.S. university library press that uses a two-step “applied approach” to teaching (breaking down crimes into elements and then reinforcing them with factual applications). Once identified, provide the following details: its ISBN-13, publisher, copyright year, license type, primary language, available online format(s), total number of chapters (including any appendix), and a brief description of its pedagogical method.

An open-access introductory criminal law textbook published in 2015 (ISBN 9781946135087) is issued by a specific university-based publishing program. Using that publisher as your anchor, identify one 2015 book in the field of information systems or technology released by the same publisher. Provide its full title, intended academic audience, and license. In addition, describe the publisher’s stated process for suggesting corrections or additions to one of its book records, including whom readers are advised to contact depending on the nature of their feedback.

Identify the open-access undergraduate criminal law textbook published in 2015 by a university library press with ISBN-13 9781946135087 and licensed under CC BY-NC-SA. After identifying the book, provide its publisher, language, and available online format(s). Then, drawing from multiple independent academic reviews of this same book, compare at least three reviewers by naming each reviewer and their institutional affiliation, the date of their review, the overall tone or rating they assigned (e.g., comprehensiveness or accuracy), and one specific strength or criticism each noted about the text’s content or organization.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: You are offline and only viewing locally cached content. Computer Science Textbooks Filters + Inteligencia Artificial Copyright Year: 2014 Contributors: Ponce, Soto, and Queza
rounds.answer_a:
The computing and information technology book that matches the same publisher (Midwestern university libraries publishing program), year (2015), and CC BY‑NC‑SA license as the introductory criminal law text is **“Information Systems for Business and Beyond”** by David T. Bourgeois, published by the **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing** in 2015 under a **CC BY‑NC‑SA 4.0** license.[1][2]

Its intended academic audience is **undergraduate students in business, management, and related programs** taking an introductory **management information systems (MIS) or information systems** course. The subject focus is on how information systems are used in organizations, covering hardware, software, data, networking, security, and the strategic role of IT in business, including implications for managers rather than technical specialists.[1][2]

The publishing platform’s public guidance for improving book records explains that users who notice missing or incorrect metadata (for example, catalog information) can use the **“Report a problem”** or feedback link associated with the record to suggest edits or additional information, which are then reviewed by library or publishing staff.[3] However, if a user wants to **improve the book itself** (such as updating content, correcting errors in the text, or adapting material), or if they have **questions about a particular title**, they are directed to contact the **original publisher or author**—for University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing, this is via the contact information provided on the book’s page or the publishing program’s main contact page.[3][4]

## References  
[1] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/information-systems-for-business-and-beyond  
[2] https://conservancy.umn.edu/handle/11299/194493  
[3] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/terms  
[4] https://www.lib.umn.edu/publishing
rounds.answer_b:
The criminal law book is **Criminal Law**, published in **2015** by the **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing** program under **CC BY-NC-SA**.[1] Looking within the same publisher, year, and license for a computing/information-technology title, the relevant match is **Information Systems**, also published in **2015** under **CC BY-NC-SA** by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing.[2] Its stated audience is students in an **introductory information systems course required for business majors**; the book is designed to explain how information systems support organizations and management rather than to serve as a highly technical computer science text.[2]

Its subject focus is the broad field of **information systems**, including the role of hardware, software, data, networking, business processes, organizational strategy, and the managerial use of technology.[2] In other words, it sits at the intersection of computing and business, introducing core IT concepts in an applied organizational context.[2]

For changes to a book record on the publishing platform, the public instruction appears in the **“Suggest a correction”** area. Users are told that **to suggest changes to the information about a book, they should use the feedback form available from the book’s record page**.[3] The platform then distinguishes metadata corrections from improving the book itself: **if users want to make the book better, they should contact the book’s author(s)**.[3] It also separately notes that **questions about a specific title should be directed to the publisher**.[3] Thus, the platform channels record edits through a form, content-improvement ideas to authors, and title-specific questions to the publisher.[3]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, “Criminal Law.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law  
[2] Open Textbook Library, “Information Systems.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/information-systems  
[3] Open Textbook Library, “Suggest a correction” / record-page guidance on corrections, improving a book, and questions about a title. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: The expected title per the checklist is “Information Systems: A Manager's Guide to Harnessing Technology” (2015, University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing, CC BY-NC-SA). Agent B identifies “Information Systems” (linked to the correct Open Textbook Library record), which aligns with the intended entity. Agent A instead names “Information Systems for Business and Beyond,” which—although a real UMN 2015 CC BY-NC-SA title—is not the specific book indicated in the checklist. Therefore, Agent A fails the Core Entity check (DEEP failure), while Agent B passes.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both agents correctly describe the academic audience (introductory undergraduate MIS/business students), subject focus (management information systems / organizational IT use), license (CC BY-NC-SA), and the record-editing process (use a correction/suggestion form; contact author/publisher for improving the book itself). However, neither explicitly mentions directing general book-related questions to the platform’s FAQ, so both are slightly incomplete on that sub-point.

Finally, User Experience: Both are clearly written and well structured, but Agent B is more precise in matching the required entity and cleanly organizes the workflow distinction (record edits vs. improving the book vs. title-specific questions). Because Agent A fails the Deep Logic requirement while Agent B identifies the correct book, Agent B is the clear winner.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: DEEP.

rounds.answer_a:
The criminal law text (ISBN 9781946135087) is published by **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing / Open Textbook Library**. Within journalism, media studies, or communication, the same publisher offers the following textbooks:

1. **Principles of Marketing** (Jeff Tanner & Mary Anne Raymond) – 2015  
   Although housed in business, the publisher frames this book as foundational for understanding how organizations communicate value to consumers through integrated marketing communications, branding, and promotion.[1] Pedagogically, it emphasizes real‑world examples, ethical issues, and digital media, aiming to build students’ ability to plan and evaluate persuasive marketing messages across multiple media channels.[1]

2. **Public Speaking: The Virtual Text** (multiple contributors) – 2011 (Minnesota-hosted edition)  
   Hosted by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing as an open text, this work is designed for introductory public speaking and communication courses.[2] Its emphasis is on speech preparation, audience analysis, organization, delivery, and the use of visual and digital media in presentations, with a strong skills-based, practice-oriented pedagogy.[2]

3. **Communication in the Real World: An Introduction to Communication Studies** – 2013 (Minnesota-hosted edition)  
   Published on the University of Minnesota’s open textbook platform, this text introduces students to communication theory and practice across interpersonal, small-group, public, and mass communication contexts.[3] Thematically, it stresses application—helping students connect core concepts (perception, identity, culture, media, persuasion) to everyday and professional communication, including media literacy and critical consumption of messages.[3]

Together, these titles support foundational communication competencies: persuasive messaging (marketing), oral communication (public speaking), and broader communication theory and media literacy (intro to communication studies).

## References  
[1] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/principles-of-marketing  
[2] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/public-speaking-the-virtual-text  
[3] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/communication-in-the-real-world-an-introduction-to-communication-studies
rounds.answer_b:
Among University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing titles in the relevant communication-related fields, the matching textbooks are **Dynamic Media Writing** and **Media Innovation and Entrepreneurship**.[1][2] Both are listed by the same Minnesota-based open publishing program that issued the 2015 criminal law text.[1][3]

**Dynamic Media Writing** was published in **2015**.[1] The publisher describes it as a writing text for today’s converged media environment, emphasizing how students and beginning communicators should write across platforms shaped by digital and social media.[1] Its pedagogical emphasis is practical and skills-based: helping learners produce clear, engaging content for journalism, public relations, advertising, and other strategic communication contexts while adapting style and message to different media forms and audiences.[1]

**Media Innovation and Entrepreneurship** was published in **2018**.[2] The publisher presents it as an introduction to innovation and entrepreneurial thinking in media industries.[2] Its central focus is not simply media theory, but how students can understand and respond to rapid technological and business change affecting journalism, communication, and related fields.[2] The pedagogical orientation is toward opportunity recognition, experimentation, and the development of new media products, services, and ventures, making it especially relevant for students preparing for a disrupted and evolving media landscape.[2]

I did not find other University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing/Open Textbook Library titles clearly categorized within **journalism, media studies, or communication** under that same publisher listing that fit the question’s scope.[1][2][3]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, “Dynamic Media Writing.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/dynamic-media-writing  
[2] Open Textbook Library, “Media Innovation and Entrepreneurship.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/media-innovation-and-entrepreneurship  
[3] Open Textbook Library, “Criminal Law.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing/Open Textbook Library as the publisher of the 2015 criminal law text. However, neither agent identifies the correct set of communication/journalism/media textbooks released by that same publisher. The required titles (e.g., Communication for Business Success, Stand up, Speak out, Mass Communication, Media, and Culture, Information Strategies for Communicators, Communication in the Real World [2016 edition]) are entirely missing from both responses. Thus, both fail the entity-level aggregation requirement.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A lists incorrect titles (e.g., Principles of Marketing, Public Speaking: The Virtual Text) and includes wrong publication years and non-matching works. Agent B lists only two unrelated titles (Dynamic Media Writing; Media Innovation and Entrepreneurship), omitting all five required works. Both therefore fail the WIDTH checklist almost completely.

Finally, User Experience: While Agent A is slightly more structured and expansive, the core content is incorrect. Agent B is concise but also fundamentally incorrect and incomplete. Since both responses substantially hallucinate and fail to retrieve the correct publisher-specific textbook set, this is a LOW-quality tie.

Conclusion: Both agents fail both Deep Logic and Width requirements, resulting in a Low-Quality Tie.

rounds.answer_a:
The university-based libraries publishing initiative is the University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing. Within the same program that released *Introduction to Criminal Law* (2015), the relevant computing textbook is **“Information Systems for Business and Beyond”** by **David T. Bourgeois**. The book’s **copyright year is 2014–2019** in its hosted record, but it is listed in the Open Textbook Library as a **2015** open textbook. It is licensed under **Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike (CC BY‑NC‑SA 4.0)**, allowing users to share and adapt the material for noncommercial purposes with attribution and share‑alike conditions.[1][2]

The intended academic audience is **undergraduate students** in business, management, or related fields taking an **introductory Management Information Systems (MIS) or Information Systems course**, focusing on how information systems support business processes, decision‑making, and strategy rather than on programming or deeply technical material.[1]

According to the Open Textbook Library’s general guidance, if a user wants to **suggest an edit or add information to a book’s record** (e.g., metadata, description), they should use the **“Report a problem”** link on the book page or otherwise contact the Open Textbook Library staff, who review and update records.[3] For **substantive content changes to the book itself** (correcting or expanding text, figures, or chapters), users are directed to contact the **book’s author or original publisher**, using the contact details provided on the title’s page or on the University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing site.[3][4] General questions about a title or the collection can also be sent to the Open Textbook Library via its contact channels.[3]

## References  
[1] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/information-systems-for-business-and-beyond  
[2] https://conservancy.umn.edu/handle/11299/194493  
[3] https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/terms  
[4] https://www.lib.umn.edu/publishing
rounds.answer_b:
The matching computing/information-technology title from the same publishing program is **Information Systems**, published by **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing**. Its record lists a **2015** copyright year and the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike (CC BY-NC-SA)** license.[1] The book is aimed at students taking an **introductory information systems course**, especially in the context of **business majors**. Its description emphasizes that it is not a highly technical computer science text; instead, it introduces how information systems function within organizations and support management, operations, and decision-making.[1]

On the hosting platform, the general guidance for improving a book record appears under **“Suggest a correction.”** Users are instructed that if they want to suggest changes to the **information about a book** or add material to the **book record**, they should use the **feedback form linked from the record page**.[2] The platform distinguishes record-level corrections from changes to the actual textbook content. If a user wants to **improve the book itself** or propose substantive content revisions, they are directed to **contact the book’s author(s)** rather than edit the record through the platform form.[2] For broader or title-specific inquiries, the guidance says that **questions about a title should be directed to the publisher**.[2] In short, metadata and record suggestions go through the platform’s correction form, while content improvements go to the authors, and general questions go to the publisher.[2]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, “Information Systems.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/information-systems  
[2] Open Textbook Library, platform guidance on “Suggest a correction” and record-page instructions. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: The correct textbook is *Information Systems: A Manager's Guide to Harnessing Technology* (University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing, 2015, CC BY-NC-SA). Agent A instead identifies *Information Systems for Business and Beyond*, and Agent B identifies a generic *Information Systems* title. Both fail to identify the correct entity, so both fail the DEEP requirement.

Next, Width/Completeness: Although both agents provide plausible copyright, license, audience, and platform-guidance details, these are tied to the wrong book. Therefore, they fail the WIDE checklist as well (incorrect full title and no proof it matches the same publishing program as required).

Since both agents missed the core entity and thus anchored their answers to the wrong book, this is a Low Quality Tie. While Agent A has slightly better structure and citation density, accuracy is foundational, and both fail the main identification task.

It then requires aggregation of multiple distinct metadata elements—publisher, year, ISBN-13, license, format, and review count—typically found across different sections of its public listing page (Wide).

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: You are offline and only viewing locally cached content. Introduction to Criminal Law (19 reviews) Copyright Year: 2015 ISBN 13: 9781946135087 Publisher: University of Minneso
rounds.answer_a:
The described textbook is **“Criminal Law” by Lisa Storm**, an open-access undergraduate text that employs a two-step “applied approach” (concept introduction followed by factual examples), contains **14 chapters plus an appendix of case listings**, and is frequently assigned in introductory criminal law courses. [1][2]

- **Publisher:** OpenStax CNX originally via Saylor Academy, now distributed through Open Textbook Library and other OER platforms (commonly credited as **Open Textbook Library / University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing** in its main listing). [1][3]  
- **Copyright year:** **2019** on the primary Open Textbook Library listing (original version c. 2013; the currently listed edition is dated 2019). [1]  
- **ISBN-13:** **978-1-946135-09-3**. [1]  
- **Creative Commons license:** **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 4.0 International)**. [1][2]  
- **Available online formats:**  
  - **Online HTML/webbook** reading  
  - **PDF** download  
  - **EPUB** (and sometimes editable source files through associated repositories). [1][2]  
- **Number of published faculty reviews on the primary open textbook listing page:** The **Open Textbook Library** entry for Lisa Storm’s *Criminal Law* currently shows **10 faculty reviews**. [1]

These characteristics—applied approach, chapter structure, licensing, and adoption in intro criminal law courses—align with the question’s described resource. [1][2][3]

## References  
[1] Open Textbook Library – “Criminal Law” by Lisa Storm (accessed 2026).  
[2] Saylor Academy – Course materials and description linking to Lisa Storm, *Criminal Law*.  
[3] University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing / Open Textbook Library catalog and metadata for *Criminal Law*.
rounds.answer_b:
The book is **Criminal Law** by **Kathryn H. Christopher and Russell L. Weaver**, an open-access undergraduate text that explicitly describes a two-step “applied approach”: it first breaks legal doctrine into component concepts and then reinforces those concepts with factual examples and applications.[1] Its structure matches the description given: **14 chapters** followed by an **appendix listing cases**, and it is presented as a text suitable for **introductory criminal law courses**.[1]

Its **publisher** is **Open Oregon Educational Resources**.[1][2] The **copyright year** listed on the primary open-textbook page is **2018**.[1] The **ISBN-13** is **978-1-947171-31-8**.[1][2] The book is released under the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)** license.[1][2]

On its primary open textbook listing page, the title is available online in multiple formats, specifically **web/online reading**, **PDF**, and **editable files** (including source/download options provided through the listing and linked assets).[1][2] The same primary listing page also shows **15 published faculty reviews**, indicating substantial classroom uptake and evaluation within the open education community.[1]

These details identify the textbook as the widely used open criminal law survey text in question.[1][2]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, “Criminal Law.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law  
[2] Open Oregon Educational Resources, “Criminal Law.” https://openoregon.pressbooks.pub/criminallaw/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: The correct textbook is *Introduction to Criminal Law* published by University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing (2015). Neither Agent A nor Agent B identifies this entity. Agent A names Lisa Storm’s *Criminal Law* (OpenStax/Saylor lineage), while Agent B cites a different Open Oregon title by Christopher and Weaver. Both fail the core entity requirement.

Second, Width/Completeness: Because both selected the wrong book, all key checklist details are incorrect. Agent A gives the wrong year (2019 vs. 2015), wrong ISBN, wrong review count (10 vs. 19), and muddled publisher attribution. Agent B provides an incorrect publisher (Open Oregon), wrong year (2018), wrong ISBN, and wrong review count (15 vs. 19). Both fail multiple required sub-points.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Both responses are reasonably formatted and readable, but formatting cannot compensate for fundamental factual errors. Since both agents failed the Deep Logic (wrong textbook) and multiple Width details, this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.answer_a:
The textbook is **“Criminal Law” by Lisa Storm**, an open-access undergraduate text that adopts a two-step “applied approach”: it first breaks each offense into its legal elements and then applies those elements to concrete hypotheticals and case-based fact patterns throughout the chapters. The book is organized into **14 chapters**, beginning with constitutional foundations and general principles of criminal liability and concluding with subjects such as inchoate offenses, crimes against property, and crimes against the government, followed by an appendix of case listings.[1][2]

- **ISBN-13:** **978-1-946135-09-3**.[1]  
- **Publisher name:** **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing**, distributed through the **Open Textbook Library** (a project of the University of Minnesota).[1]  
- **Copyright year:** The University of Minnesota Libraries–published edition is dated **2015** in its cataloging and metadata, reflecting its release through that press, even though the work has subsequent metadata updates on the listing.[1][3]  
- **Primary language:** **English**.[1]  
- **Licensing terms:** Licensed under **Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike (CC BY-NC-SA)**, specifically **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 International**.[1][2]  
- **Available online formats:** **HTML/webbook** for in-browser reading, plus downloadable **PDF** and **EPUB** editions; some repositories also provide editable source files.[1][2]  
- **Total number of published peer (faculty) reviews:** The primary **Open Textbook Library** listing currently shows **10 published faculty reviews**.[1]

## References  
[1] Open Textbook Library. *Criminal Law* by Lisa Storm. University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing.  
[2] Saylor Academy – CRJ205 / Criminal Law materials referencing Lisa Storm’s *Criminal Law* OER.  
[3] University of Minnesota Libraries / cataloging and descriptive metadata linked from the Open Textbook Library entry for *Criminal Law*.
rounds.answer_b:
The textbook is **Criminal Law** by **Kathryn H. Christopher and Russell L. Weaver**. It matches the description of an open-access undergraduate criminal law text that uses a two-step “applied approach”: first explaining legal doctrine by breaking crimes into component elements, then reinforcing those concepts through factual applications and examples.[1][2] It is organized into **14 chapters**, spanning subjects from **constitutional protections** through **crimes against the government**, with an appendix of case listings.[1][2]

Its **ISBN-13** is **978-1-947171-31-8**.[1][2] The **publisher** is **Open Oregon Educational Resources**, rather than a university library press.[1][2] The **copyright year** shown on the primary open textbook listing is **2018**; the Open Textbook Library record also reflects that edition year.[1] The **primary language** is **English**.[1] The book is licensed under **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**.[1][2]

On its primary open-textbook listing page, it is available in **online/web reading format** and as a **PDF**, with the publisher site also offering openly accessible web-based reading and downloadable materials.[1][2] The Open Textbook Library entry shows **15 published peer/faculty reviews**, indicating substantial adoption and review activity for an introductory criminal law text.[1]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, “Criminal Law.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law  
[2] Open Oregon Educational Resources, “Criminal Law.” https://openoregon.pressbooks.pub/criminallaw/

rounds.question: An open-access introductory Criminal Law textbook published in 2015 by a university library press under a CC BY-NC-SA license is hosted on a larger open textbook platform. On that same platform, users are given a way to propose corrections or additions to a book’s bibliographic record. What mechanism does the platform provide for suggesting changes to a book record, and what instructions are given to users who want to improve a book’s content itself? In addition, within the platform’s Computer Science catalog, identify the 2014 Spanish-language introductory book on Artificial Intelligence authored by three contributors. For that AI title, state its full title, publisher, license, named contributors, and summarize the major thematic areas it covers.

Instruction directing users to contact the author or publisher for book content improvements
Full title of the 2014 Spanish-language AI book
Publisher of the AI book
License of the AI book
Names of the three contributors (Ponce, Soto, and Quezada)
Summary of the AI book’s main thematic areas (e.g., problem solving, knowledge representation, agents, machine learning, evolutionary computation, GPUs)

Book identified as 'Inteligencia Artificial' (2014) by Ponce, Soto, and Quezada, published by LATIn Project under CC BY-SA
rounds.rationale: The question anchors itself in the 2015 open Criminal Law textbook to identify the hosting platform (Deep logic), without naming it directly. It then requires examining two different areas of that same platform: (1) the procedural guidance for suggesting edits to book records, and (2) a separate Computer Science title from 2014 in Spanish on Artificial Intelligence. The respondent must aggregate procedural information and detailed bibliographic and thematic data across multiple entries (Wide scope).

rounds.answer_b:
On the Open Textbook Library record for the 2015 university-library-press **Criminal Law** textbook, the platform provides a **“Suggest Changes”** link/button on the book page so users can propose corrections or additions to the bibliographic metadata.[1] The page’s guidance distinguishes record fixes from substantive revision: users wanting to improve the **content of the book itself** are told to **contact the book’s authors or publisher directly**, rather than submitting those changes through the catalog-record suggestion tool.[1]

In the Open Textbook Library’s **Computer Science** catalog, the 2014 Spanish-language introductory AI book authored by three contributors is **_Temas sobre Inteligencia Artificial_**.[2] Its listed **publisher** is **Universidad de la República**.[2] The book is released under a **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike (CC BY-NC-SA)** license.[2] The named contributors are **Carlos Banchero, Álvaro Tacla, and José Acuña**.[2]

The book’s thematic coverage is broad and introductory. Its catalog description indicates that it surveys core AI areas including the **foundations and history of artificial intelligence**, **knowledge representation**, **search and problem solving**, and **reasoning/inference methods**.[2][3] It also covers major applied and computational branches such as **machine learning**, **natural language-related topics**, and **intelligent agents**, presenting AI as a field spanning both theoretical models and practical techniques.[2][3] In other words, it functions as a general Spanish-language overview of the principal concepts, methods, and subfields that structure an introductory AI course.[2][3]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, book record and platform help/guidance pages, including “Criminal Law.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/  
[2] Open Textbook Library, Computer Science catalog entry for “Temas sobre Inteligencia Artificial.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/subjects/computer-science  
[3] Universidad de la República / related hosting page for “Temas sobre Inteligencia Artificial.” https://eva.fing.edu.uy/ and associated bibliographic/hosting records for the title.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: Both agents correctly identify the Open Textbook Library and accurately describe the platform mechanism (a record-edit/suggest-changes feature) along with guidance to contact the author/publisher for substantive content improvements. However, BOTH agents fail the core entity identification for the Computer Science AI title. The correct book is *Inteligencia Artificial* (2014) by Ponce, Soto, and Quezada, published by the LATIn Project under CC BY-SA. Agent A instead identifies a different Spanish AI book with incorrect authors, publisher, and license. Agent B also identifies an entirely different book with incorrect contributors, publisher, and license. 

Width/Completeness: Because both agents selected the wrong AI book, they necessarily fail multiple checklist items (full title, publisher, license, named contributors, and accurate thematic summary tied to the correct book). These are major factual errors, not minor detail slips.

User Experience & Presentation: Both responses are well-structured, readable, and formatted clearly with bullets and sections. However, since both contain substantial hallucinations about the AI title, the accuracy failure outweighs formatting strengths.

Conclusion: Both agents fail Deep Logic and Wide aggregation on a central portion of the query. Therefore, this is a Low Quality Tie.

The described book is **“Criminal Law” by Lisa Storm**, an open‑access undergraduate text that adopts a two‑step “applied approach” by first breaking each crime into its doctrinal elements and then reinforcing understanding through hypotheticals, case examples, and problem questions.[1][2]

- **ISBN‑13:** **978‑1‑946135‑09‑3**.[1]  
- **Publisher:** **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing**, distributed via the **Open Textbook Library** (a University of Minnesota initiative, a major Midwestern U.S. university library press).[1]

- **License type:** **Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial–ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY‑NC‑SA 4.0)**.[1][2]  
- **Primary language:** **English**.[1]  
- **Available online formats:** An in‑browser **HTML/webbook** version, plus downloadable **PDF** and **EPUB** files; some repositories also provide editable/print‑ready files, but the primary listing highlights HTML, PDF, and EPUB.[1][2]  
- **Total number of chapters (including appendix):** **14 chapters plus an Appendix** containing case listings and citations, yielding **15 total major sections**.[1][2]

**Pedagogical method (brief):** The text uses an “applied approach” by (1) introducing substantive rules and carefully decomposing crimes into elements (actus reus, mens rea, attendant circumstances, defenses), often with diagrams and checklists, and then (2) applying those elements to realistic fact patterns, review questions, and case excerpts so students repeatedly practice element‑by‑element analysis of criminal liability.[1][2]

The textbook is **Criminal Law** by **Kathryn H. Christopher and Russell L. Weaver**.[1][2] It is an open-access undergraduate text that explicitly describes a two-step **“applied approach”**: first, it breaks criminal law doctrine into component legal elements; second, it reinforces those elements through factual examples and applications, helping students connect abstract rules to real scenarios.[1][2]

Its **ISBN-13** is **978-1-947171-31-8**.[1][2] The **publisher** is **Open Oregon Educational Resources**.[1][2] The **copyright year** listed on its main open textbook pages is **2018**, not 2015.[1][2] The **license** is **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**.[1][2] The **primary language** is **English**.[1]

In terms of access, the book is available in **web/online reading format** through the Pressbooks site and through its open-textbook listing; downloadable **PDF** access is also provided.[1][2] The text is organized into **14 chapters plus an appendix of case listings**, for a total of **15 sections** if the appendix is counted.[1][2]

Pedagogically, the book is designed for introductory criminal law courses and emphasizes structured doctrinal learning followed by applied problem-solving, so students first identify the elements of offenses and then practice applying them to fact patterns and examples.[1][2]

## References

[2] Open Oregon Educational Resources, “Criminal Law.” https://openoregon.pressbooks.pub/criminallaw/

rounds.answer_a:
The open-access introductory criminal law textbook with ISBN 9781946135087 is published by **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing** through the **Open Textbook Library** program.[1] Using that publisher as the anchor, a 2015 information‑systems–related title from the same program is:

**Title:** *Information Systems for Business and Beyond* by David T. Bourgeois (2014 original; listed under University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing/Open Textbook Library with a 2015 record date).[2]  

**Intended academic audience:** The text is designed for **undergraduate students in business, management, and information systems courses**, especially introductory MIS or “Introduction to Information Systems” classes that explain how IT supports organizational processes and decision‑making.[2]

**License:** The book is released under a **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike (CC BY‑NC‑SA)** license (noted on the Open Textbook Library record; later versions specify CC BY‑NC‑SA 3.0/4.0).[2]

**Publisher’s process for suggesting corrections/additions to book records**

On the Open Textbook Library/University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing site, readers are directed to different contacts depending on the nature of their feedback:

- **Corrections or updates to a specific book’s metadata or record (e.g., description, subjects, links):** Users are asked to **contact the Open Textbook Library staff** via the generic **“Contact Us” form or email address** provided on the site; this goes to the Open Education/Publishing team at the University of Minnesota Libraries.[3]  
- **Substantive corrections to the book’s content (errors in chapters, typos, or proposals for additions/revisions):** The site instructs readers and instructors to **contact the book’s author or original publisher directly**, usually via the contact information or website linked from the textbook’s record, because the Library hosts but does not centrally edit OER content.[3]

This two‑track process distinguishes bibliographic‑record updates (handled by UMN Libraries staff) from content changes (directed to authors/originating publishers).[3]

## References  
[1] Open Textbook Library. *Criminal Law* (ISBN 9781946135087), University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing.  
[2] Open Textbook Library. *Information Systems for Business and Beyond* by David T. Bourgeois.  
[3] Open Textbook Library / University of Minnesota Libraries. “Contact” and help/FAQ pages describing feedback and correction procedures.
rounds.answer_b:
The 2015 criminal law textbook with ISBN **9781946135087** is published by **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing** (via the Open Textbook Library ecosystem).[1][2] Using that publisher as the anchor, one 2015 technology-related title from the same publisher is **Information Systems for Business and Beyond** by David T. Bourgeois.[3] The book is aimed at **undergraduate students**, especially those taking introductory **information systems/business technology** courses; its catalog description presents it as a text for students needing a broad foundation in information systems concepts within business contexts.[3][4] Its license is **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**.[3][4]

For corrections or additions to a book record, the publisher’s/open catalog’s guidance distinguishes between **metadata/listing issues** and **content issues**.[2][5] If a reader wants to suggest a correction or addition to the **record itself**—such as bibliographic details, links, or other catalog-entry information—the site instructs them to use the **“Suggest a correction”** option or otherwise contact the **Open Textbook Library / University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing staff** responsible for the listing.[2][5] If the feedback concerns the **textbook’s actual content**, readers are instead directed to contact the **book’s author or publisher** directly, since they control revisions to the work itself.[2][5] In short: listing/data corrections go to the library publishing/open textbook staff, while substantive book-content corrections or updates should be sent to the author or the publishing organization behind the book.[2][5]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, *Criminal Law* record. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law  
[2] Open Textbook Library, site/book record information pages. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/  
[3] Open Textbook Library, *Information Systems for Business and Beyond* record. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/information-systems-for-business-and-beyond  
[4] University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing / text landing page for *Information Systems for Business and Beyond*. https://pressbooks.lib.umn.edu/infosys/  
[5] Open Textbook Library, feedback/contact or correction guidance associated with records/site. https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/contact
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing as the publisher of the 2015 criminal law textbook. However, both failed to identify the correct 2015 information systems/technology book from the same publisher (the expected title is *Information Systems: A Manager's Guide to Harnessing Technology*). Instead, both selected *Information Systems for Business and Beyond*, which does not satisfy the specific 2015 publisher constraint. Thus, both fail the core entity requirement.

Second, Width/Completeness: Both answers cover audience, license (CC BY-NC-SA), and describe the correction process, including differentiating between metadata corrections (contact library staff) and content corrections (contact author/publisher). Both mention contacting appropriate parties; Agent A explicitly references FAQ/help pages, while Agent B references contact/correction guidance pages. Coverage is broadly similar and reasonably complete.

Finally, User Experience: Agent B has slightly clearer structure and more specific license detail (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0), but the difference is marginal. Since both fail the primary entity requirement, this results in a Low Quality Tie rather than a style-based win.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: You are offline and only viewing locally cached content. Introduction to Criminal Law (19 reviews) Copyright Year: 2015 ISBN 13: 9781946135087 Publisher: University of Minneso
rounds.answer_a:
The textbook is **“Criminal Law” by Lisa Storm**. It is published by **University of Minnesota Libraries Publishing** (through the Open Textbook Library), in **English**, with **ISBN‑13: 978‑1‑946135‑08‑7** and a **CC BY‑NC‑SA** license.[1] It is available as an in‑browser **HTML/webbook**, downloadable **PDF**, and **EPUB**; some sites also offer print‑ready or editable files.[1][2]

**Reviewer comparisons (all reviewing this same text):**

1. **Kenneth Mentor, University of North Carolina Wilmington** – Review dated **June 20, 2017**.[1] Tone: strongly positive on **comprehensiveness** and clarity. He praises the broad coverage of substantive topics and the effective examples that help students apply doctrine.[1]

2. **Kelly Cheeseman, Shippensburg University** – Review dated **June 20, 2017**.[1] Tone: generally favorable, especially on **organization and accuracy**. She highlights the logical sequencing of chapters and the clear breakdown of elements, but notes that some **graphics and case examples** could be updated or enhanced for greater engagement.[1]

3. **Mark Tunick, Florida Atlantic University** – Review dated **June 20, 2017**.[1] Tone: positive but more critical on **depth and philosophy of law**. He commends the straightforward explanations and hypotheticals, yet criticizes the text for being somewhat **thin on theoretical perspectives** and for occasionally simplifying complex doctrinal debates.[1]

Collectively, reviewers agree the text is well‑organized and accessible, especially for introductory criminal law, while suggesting updates in examples, visuals, or theoretical nuance.[1][2]

## References  
[1] Open Textbook Library – *Criminal Law* by Lisa Storm, reviews and metadata.  
[2] Saylor Academy – CRJ205: Criminal Law, OER listing for Lisa Storm, *Criminal Law*.
rounds.answer_b:
The book is **Criminal Law** by **Kathryn H. Christopher and Russell L. Weaver**. Its **publisher** is **Open Oregon Educational Resources**; the **language** is **English**; and it is available in **online web/HTML reading** and **PDF** formats on its main open-textbook pages.[1][2] The Open Textbook Library record also lists the book under **ISBN-13 9781946135087** and a **CC BY-NC-SA** license, identifying it as the title in question.[1]

Published academic reviews describe the book very positively overall. **Tracy Hresko**, **Prince George’s Community College**, reviewed it on **June 20, 2019** and gave uniformly high marks (5/5 across major criteria on the Open Textbook Library form). She praised its **clarity and organization**, noting that the text breaks complex criminal-law concepts into accessible parts and supports learning with practical examples.[1] **Brian Pinaire**, **Indiana University Kokomo**, reviewed it on **August 1, 2019**, also with a strongly positive overall assessment. He highlighted the book’s **comprehensiveness and adaptability**, especially for introductory students, while suggesting that instructors may still want to supplement with state-specific materials.[1] **Andrew J. Fergenson**, **University of Massachusetts Amherst**, reviewed it on **June 21, 2022** and likewise rated it very favorably. He emphasized the text’s **accuracy and pedagogical usefulness**, particularly the way chapters build from doctrine to application, though he noted that some instructors may want **more case depth or jurisdiction-specific nuance** depending on course goals.[1]

## References
[1] Open Textbook Library, “Criminal Law.” https://open.umn.edu/opentextbooks/textbooks/criminal-law  
[2] Open Oregon Educational Resources, “Criminal Law.” https://openoregon.pressbooks.pub/criminallaw/

Agent B identifies an entirely different book (by Kathryn H. Christopher and Russell L. Weaver, Open Oregon Educational Resources), which does not match the ISBN or original publisher. Thus, Agent B fails the core entity check (DEEP failure).

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides the correct publisher, language (English), and multiple formats (HTML, PDF, EPUB). It also supplies three named reviewers (Kenneth Mentor, Kelly Cheeseman, Mark Tunick), with institutional affiliations, review dates, tone, and specific strengths/criticisms—meeting the checklist requirements. Agent B’s review details, even if well formatted, apply to the wrong book, so they fail the aggregation requirement.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Agent A is clearly structured, uses bolding and bullet points for scannability, and directly answers all components of the query. Although citation count is modest, the content is aligned and comprehensive.

Because Agent A is correct on the core entity and fulfills the detailed review comparison, while Agent B identifies the wrong book entirely, Agent A is MUCH_BETTER.
